title: Intersight Firmware Compliance — UCS, X-Series, and Fabric Interconnects
exclusions: Estates that remain on UCS Manager–only workflows without Intersight claim or Intersight Connected mode never emit firmwarerunningfirmware to your Splunk index; they belong under classic UCS TAs and a different set of CIMC or UCSM object searches. Third-party NIC, GPU, or FC HBA images that are not modeled as Intersight RunningFirmware for your subscription will be invisible here even when they are business-critical—track those in a host-based or vendor-specific add-on, not in this path. Intentional BIOS or policy deviation tickets that are approved outside the lookup row (true exceptions) will still alert unless you add an exclusion column keyed on serial; do not conflate that with a product defect. Pure BIOS settings drift (voltage, boot order) without a version change is a configuration drift problem, not firmware equality. HCX, standalone CIMC, or non-Cisco x86 under the same dashboard cannot be compared with UCS model strings. Air-gapped Intersight Appliance deployments where API export is blocked to Splunk for policy reasons are out of scope until the data path exists. NVMe and drive firmware on carriers that do not report through Intersight in your tenant will require edge collection—this UC’s join keys will not see them. Finally, if you use only upgrade-status MOs and never turn on firmware.RunningFirmware, the index will be empty by design; fix inputs before blaming SPL.
dataSources: `index=cisco_intersight` (or the index your modular input targets) and sourcetype `cisco:intersight:firmwarerunningfirmware` for per-component `Version` strings on servers, IOM, FI, adapters, and drives as Cisco exposes them. Cross-check model and serial through `cisco:intersight:computephysicalsummary`, optional HCL context through `cisco:intersight:hclstatus`, repository metadata through `cisco:intersight:firmwaredistributable` when you add dashboards, and `cisco:intersight:firmwarepolicy` when you want to compare an assigned policy bundle to live PackageVersion. Ingested by `Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight` (Splunkbase 5953) using OAuth2 client credentials; each Intersight class maps to a lowercased sourcetype prefix `cisco:intersight` plus the MO name without dots.
app: Install the Splunk Add-on for Cisco Intersight on disk as `Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight` ([Splunkbase 5953](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5953)). Enterprise 9.x and 10.x and Splunk Cloud are supported in current TA builds; pin to a version your security team reviewed and read release notes for field renames.
spl:
index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:firmwarerunningfirmware" earliest=-7d@d
| eval server_moid = coalesce('Server.Moid', server_moid, ServerMoid, parent_moid)
| eval server_name = coalesce('Server.Name', server_name, Description, "unknown")
| eval component = coalesce(Component, component, Type, "unknown")
| eval running_version = coalesce(Version, version, "unknown")
| eval mode = coalesce(Mode, mode, "running")
| where mode == "running" AND running_version != "unknown"
| stats latest(_time) as last_seen,
        latest(running_version) as running_version,
        latest(PackageVersion) as bundle_version
        by server_moid, server_name, component
| join type=left server_moid 
    [ search index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:computephysicalsummary" earliest=-7d@d
    | eval server_moid = coalesce(Moid, moid)
    | stats latest(Model) as model, latest(Serial) as serial, latest(Name) as registered_name by server_moid ]
| eval model = coalesce(model, "unknown")
| lookup intersight_approved_firmware model component OUTPUT approved_version baseline_bundle end_of_support_date cve_count
| eval drift = if(isnotnull(approved_version) AND running_version != approved_version, "yes", "no")
| eval hcl_outdated = if(isnotnull(end_of_support_date) AND end_of_support_date < strftime(now(), "%Y-%m-%d"), "yes", "no")
| eval severity_bucket = case(
    drift == "yes" AND coalesce(cve_count, 0) >= 1, "critical",
    drift == "yes" AND hcl_outdated == "yes", "high",
    drift == "yes", "medium",
    hcl_outdated == "yes", "medium",
    1==1, "low")
| where drift == "yes" OR hcl_outdated == "yes"
| sort +severity_bucket, server_name, component
| eval drilldown = "https://www.intersight.com/an/operate/server/" . server_moid . "/inventory/system"
| table last_seen, server_name, model, serial, component, running_version, approved_version, baseline_bundle, drift, hcl_outdated, cve_count, severity_bucket, drilldown
description: Joins the last running firmware per server component from `cisco:intersight:firmwarerunningfirmware` with `cisco:intersight:computephysicalsummary` to recover Model/Serial, then looks up the customer-maintained `intersight_approved_firmware` table by (model, component) and flags any string where `Version` differs from the approved value or where the lookup’s `end_of_support_date` is already past, bucketed into a severity case that also considers a numeric cve_count cell.
value: You catch B-Series, C-Series, X-Series, Fabric Interconnect, and IOM code that is off your golden path before silent security exposure, failed change audits, or unplanned reboot drift during failover exercises; the business sees fewer emergency firmware weekends, lower audit findings on patch posture, and faster evidence when regulators or insurers ask for proof of controlled baselines on Cisco compute.
implementation: Install Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight, confirm firmwarerunningfirmware and computephysicalsummary events land in cisco_intersight, install a small `intersight-firmware-compliance` app holding the `intersight_approved_firmware` lookup, paste the `spl` into a scheduled report, and add alerts on `severity_bucket` of critical and high with throttles on server_moid+component. Rebuild the lookup after every Cisco infrastructure bundle you adopt.
visualization: A sortable `table` of drift rows with `severity_bucket`, a bar chart of counts by `model`, a single value for percent of touched servers in drift, a time chart of new drift discoveries per day, a parallel panel using `cisco:intersight:hclstatus` for HardwareStatus/SoftwareStatus overlays, and a second drilldown `table` for Fabric Interconnect and IOM rows where `component` is system. Include a deep link column from the `drilldown` eval in the saved SPL.

references.title: Cisco Intersight REST API — introduction / overview
Cisco Intersight — support and software downloads hub

knownFalsePositives:
• RunningFirmware rows may lag a completed upgrade if the next poll has not run—expect a 4–6 hour window after a change before drift clears, so pair alerts with cisco:intersight:firmwareupgradestatus in dashboards while treating single-shot alerts as “still converging”.
• Model strings that differ only by marketing suffix (UCSB-B200-M6-U vs UCSB-B200-M6) can miss a lookup join until you normalise or duplicate rows; fix the lookup, not the server.
• Some components list Version as a bundle string while the lookup stores a shorter approved token from an older runbook; align both sides to the exact Intersight output after each Cisco release, or the eval marks drift on formatting alone.
• Lab or spare blades that are intentionally on a retired image for triage of old bugs will false-positive against a production strict CSV until you add a second lookup table tagged nonprod with relaxed rows.
• FI and IOM rows sometimes share a Server relationship pattern that is null while RegisteredDevice is populated; coalesce paths that skip your join are not “green” hardware—they need props tuning or a follow-up search.
• cve_count is operator-maintained; if the cell is empty, coalesce in SPL treats the row as low severity, which can under-page real PSIRT work—treat null cve_count as a content defect, not a clean bill of health.
• HCL and RunningFirmware can disagree for a short window when Cisco reclassifies support; cross-check hclstatus before you emergency-patch healthy hosts.
• Hot-spare parts swapped from stock may show a newer firmware than your golden label; decide policy: either widen approved_version or track those serials in an exception file.
• When Intersight reports a backup or secondary image mode during failover tests, the SPL filters to mode==running; do not expect alerts from passive banks unless you add a second search for that purpose.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
Scope: Cisco Intersight–claimed Cisco UCS B-Series (including B200 M5, B480 M5), C-Series (C220, C240, C480 M5 through M7 generations), X-Series (X210c M6 and M7, X410c M7), 6300/6400/6500 family Fabric Interconnects such as 6332, 6332-16UP, 6454, 64108, and 6536, and matching IOM 2208XP, 2304, 2408, and 2424 modules, plus storage components whose firmware is exposed through Intersight including rotating-media and NVMe devices when the Intersight MO lists them. You need a Heavy Forwarder or equivalent running Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight (Splunkbase 5953) with an OAuth2 API key to your tenant, an approved index (commonly cisco_intersight), and a custom app to host the intersight_approved_firmware lookup. Network egress to intersight.com (or the hostname your account documents) is mandatory; proxy and TLS inspection misconfiguration are common root causes of empty data. The TA maps each Intersight managed-object class to a lowercased sourcetype: the firmware.RunningFirmware class becomes cisco:intersight:firmwarerunningfirmware, so the primary signal for live versions is the sourcetype string and the JSON body, not a synthetic object_type= filter. Keep index-time and search-time in one time zone: Intersight timestamps are usually UTC, and mixing local-time comparisons with UTC lookup dates will make end_of_support_date checks appear off by a day at boundaries.

## Step 1 Onboard Intersight Firmware Telemetry

Install Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight on the forwarder that will hold the modular input and on Search Heads that need interactive field discovery. In Intersight, open Settings, API Keys, and create a new key pair. Choose a role with read access to inventory and firmware; Account Administrator and Server Administrator–style read scopes are typical when your security team will not hand Organisation Administrator to an integration. Copy KeyId and Secret into the add-on’s account workflow so Splunk stores them under passwords.conf, not in cleartext Git.

Enable collection for firmware and inventory MO classes. At minimum, poll firmware.RunningFirmware so you have per-component version strings, firmware.Distributable and firmware.Policy if you want repository and policy context in dashboards, firmware.UpgradeStatus to correlate in-flight upgrades, hcl.Status for published compatibility posture, and compute.PhysicalSummary to recover Model and Serial. The modular input in your build may use mo_class_filter or the closest vendor spelling—mirror the default stanza names from the shipped default/inputs.conf, then override in local. A six-hour polling window (21600 seconds) is a sensible starting point: firmware is slow-moving and Intersight rate-limits API traffic (plan for roughly 100 requests per minute per account as a design guardrail; split lab and production keys if internal scripts also call the same tenant).

Bind destination index to cisco_intersight (or the name your organisation standardises) and set sourcetype_prefix cisco:intersight so the resulting sourcetype for FirmwareRunningFirmware is exactly cisco:intersight:firmwarerunningfirmware. If you rename in props, update every search and alias together during a single change window.

Reference inputs.conf (adapt stanza names to your TA version, never copy blind):

```ini
# /opt/splunk/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight/local/inputs.conf
# Heavy Forwarder polling Intersight SaaS via OAuth2

[intersight://intersight_prod_account]
account = intersight_prod_account
endpoint = https://intersight.com/api/v1
mo_class_filter = firmware.RunningFirmware,firmware.Distributable,firmware.UpgradeStatus,firmware.Policy,hcl.Status,compute.PhysicalSummary,compute.Blade,compute.RackUnit
polling_interval = 21600
disabled = 0
sourcetype_prefix = cisco:intersight
index = cisco_intersight
```

Reference passwords.conf (values come from the setup UI, not hand-typed secrets):

```ini
# /opt/splunk/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight/local/passwords.conf
[credential::intersight_prod_account]
api_key_id = <encrypted>
secret_key = <encrypted>
```

After two poll cycles, run a count probe: `index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:firmwarerunningfirmware" earliest=-1d | stats count by Component` — you should see non-zero volume for system, adapter, and management-plane style components. If the count is zero, verify DNS, API scope, and that the target devices are Intersight-claimed, not only UCS Manager–local.

## Step 2 Parse and Normalize

Place TA-level props for JSON indexed extractions, UTC timestamps, and long-line handling. The FirmwareRunningFirmware stream carries nested relationship objects: Server.Moid, Server.Name, and Component, Version, Mode, and PackageVersion matter for joins. In mixed TA releases, you may also see moid/serial without dots—hence the coalesce-rich SPL, not a brittle single field. Field aliases reduce dashboard typing but the saved search in this use case is defensive and repeats coalesce in-line so upgrades do not break night alerts.

props.conf (merge into the TA or a thin wrapper app; test with `btool`):

```ini
[cisco:intersight:firmwarerunningfirmware]
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
KV_MODE = none
TIMESTAMP_FIELDS = ModTime,ModifiedTime,_indextime
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%3NZ
TZ = UTC
TRUNCATE = 0
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
FIELDALIAS-server = Server.Moid AS server_moid Server.Name AS server_name
FIELDALIAS-component = Component AS component Version AS running_version PackageVersion AS bundle_version
description = Cisco Intersight FirmwareRunningFirmware MO snapshot per component

[cisco:intersight:hclstatus]
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
KV_MODE = none
TIMESTAMP_FIELDS = ModTime
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%3NZ
TZ = UTC
TRUNCATE = 0
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
FIELDALIAS-server = Server.Moid AS server_moid
FIELDALIAS-status = Status AS hcl_status HardwareStatus AS hw_status SoftwareStatus AS sw_status

[cisco:intersight:computephysicalsummary]
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
KV_MODE = none
TIMESTAMP_FIELDS = ModTime
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%3NZ
TZ = UTC
TRUNCATE = 0
FIELDALIAS-id = Moid AS server_moid
FIELDALIAS-inv = Model AS model Serial AS serial Name AS registered_name OperState AS oper_state
```

Define the organisation-owned baseline in intersight_approved_firmware. Columns are model, component, approved_version, baseline_bundle, end_of_support_date, cve_count. Populate model to match the Model field from compute.PhysicalSummary (UCSB-B200-M6 style), and normalise component labels to match the Component or Type you see in firmwarerunningfirmware (examples: system, BIOS, BMC, IOM, adapter, storage-controller, disk). approved_version is the string equality target for Version; when Cisco publishes non-normalised vendor strings, store the exact Intersight-reported value in the lookup rather than inventing a second format. baseline_bundle is the human change-ticket name you expect operators to reference. end_of_support_date is ISO 8601 date for EOS comparisons in SPL. cve_count is a numeric cell you can start from PSIRT review or from an internal register (zero means “tracked, none recorded”). Source rows from your golden-image catalogue after each bundle drop, and optionally merge hints from the public HCL API (for example the Supported Firmware Releases family) on a schedule you can defend to auditors. Wildcard only when your transforms.conf sets match_type WILDCARD and you are deliberate about false matches.

lookup transforms.conf:

```ini
# /opt/splunk/etc/apps/intersight-firmware-compliance/local/transforms.conf
[intersight_approved_firmware]
filename = intersight_approved_firmware.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
min_matches = 1
match_type = WILDCARD(model) WILDCARD(component)
```

Initial CSV sample (copy to $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/intersight-firmware-compliance/lookups/ and set permissions to the search role):

```csv
# /opt/splunk/etc/apps/intersight-firmware-compliance/lookups/intersight_approved_firmware.csv
model,component,approved_version,baseline_bundle,end_of_support_date,cve_count
UCSB-B200-M5,system,4.2(3l),4.2(3l),2026-12-31,2
UCSB-B200-M5,BIOS,B200M5.4.2.3l.0.0810230030,4.2(3l),2026-12-31,0
UCSB-B200-M5,BMC,4.2(3l),4.2(3l),2026-12-31,1
UCSB-B200-M6,system,4.3(2.230207),4.3(2.230207),2028-12-31,0
UCSB-B200-M7,system,5.1(0.230054),5.1(0.230054),2030-12-31,0
UCSC-C220-M5,system,4.2(3i),4.2(3i),2026-12-31,3
UCSC-C220-M6,system,4.3(2.230207),4.3(2.230207),2028-12-31,0
UCSC-C220-M7,system,5.1(0.230054),5.1(0.230054),2030-12-31,0
UCSX-210C-M6,system,5.1(0.230054),5.1(0.230054),2030-12-31,0
UCSX-210C-M7,system,5.2(0.230092),5.2(0.230092),2032-12-31,0
UCS-FI-6332,system,9.3(5)I43(7c),3.2(3o)A,2026-06-30,4
UCS-FI-6454,system,9.3(5)I43(7c),4.2(3l)A,2028-06-30,1
UCS-FI-64108,system,9.3(5)I43(7c),4.2(3l)A,2028-06-30,1
UCS-FI-6536,system,9.3(5)I43(7c),4.3(2.230207)A,2030-06-30,0
UCS-IOM-2408,system,4.2(3l),4.2(3l),2028-12-31,0
UCS-IOM-2424,system,5.1(0.230054),5.1(0.230054),2030-12-31,0
```

Wire default.meta so system users and your compliance role can read the lookup, and add automatic CLEANUP=1 only after you test joins during upgrades.

## Step 3 Validate

Validation is intentionally different from a pure alarm count check: you are proving that firmware objects, model joins, and policy rows line up, not that Intersight raised a cond/Alarms row.

(a) API access without Splunk: obtain a short-lived OAuth token using your API key, then `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <oauth_token>" 'https://intersight.com/api/v1/firmware/RunningFirmwares?$top=5'`. You should see JSON with ClassId firmware.RunningFirmware, Version, and Server relationships. 401/403 means scope or key rotation, not a Splunk problem yet.

(b) Splunk parse proof: `index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:firmwarerunningfirmware" earliest=-1d | stats count by Component | head 20` confirms indexed JSON plus poll cadence, and the distribution should echo what you see in the Intersight Operate view for a known server. Pair with `| fieldsummary Version` to catch truncation—if you see uniform short strings, check TRUNCATE in props on the forwarder tier.

(c) Controlled drift: pick a non-production server in a change-approved window, set its firmware in Intersight to a bundle your lookup deliberately marks as disallowed (or lower the approved_version field for a lab row only), wait at least one poll (up to six hours with default cadence), then re-run the saved search. You want drift=yes with severity at least medium, without touching unrelated hosts. Revert the device and restore the approved lookup row the same day.

(d) Join confidence: from Search, run a narrow subsearch: `index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:computephysicalsummary" earliest=-1d | eval server_moid=coalesce(Moid,moid) | stats latest(Model) as model, latest(Serial) as serial by server_moid | join server_moid [search index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:firmwarerunningfirmware" earliest=-1d | eval server_moid=coalesce('Server.Moid',server_moid) | stats latest(Version) as v by server_moid] | where isnotnull(model) AND isnotnull(v)`; every claimed production blade should have both sides, or you have a claim/inventory gap to fix before you trust a compliance report.

After behaviour checks, keep the report version locked to the SPL in the spl field, embedded here for the operator copy/paste path:

```spl
index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:firmwarerunningfirmware" earliest=-7d@d
| eval server_moid = coalesce('Server.Moid', server_moid, ServerMoid, parent_moid)
| eval server_name = coalesce('Server.Name', server_name, Description, "unknown")
| eval component = coalesce(Component, component, Type, "unknown")
| eval running_version = coalesce(Version, version, "unknown")
| eval mode = coalesce(Mode, mode, "running")
| where mode == "running" AND running_version != "unknown"
| stats latest(_time) as last_seen,
        latest(running_version) as running_version,
        latest(PackageVersion) as bundle_version
        by server_moid, server_name, component
| join type=left server_moid 
    [ search index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:computephysicalsummary" earliest=-7d@d
    | eval server_moid = coalesce(Moid, moid)
    | stats latest(Model) as model, latest(Serial) as serial, latest(Name) as registered_name by server_moid ]
| eval model = coalesce(model, "unknown")
| lookup intersight_approved_firmware model component OUTPUT approved_version baseline_bundle end_of_support_date cve_count
| eval drift = if(isnotnull(approved_version) AND running_version != approved_version, "yes", "no")
| eval hcl_outdated = if(isnotnull(end_of_support_date) AND end_of_support_date < strftime(now(), "%Y-%m-%d"), "yes", "no")
| eval severity_bucket = case(
    drift == "yes" AND coalesce(cve_count, 0) >= 1, "critical",
    drift == "yes" AND hcl_outdated == "yes", "high",
    drift == "yes", "medium",
    hcl_outdated == "yes", "medium",
    1==1, "low")
| where drift == "yes" OR hcl_outdated == "yes"
| sort +severity_bucket, server_name, component
| eval drilldown = "https://www.intersight.com/an/operate/server/" . server_moid . "/inventory/system"
| table last_seen, server_name, model, serial, component, running_version, approved_version, baseline_bundle, drift, hcl_outdated, cve_count, severity_bucket, drilldown
```

## Step 4 Operationalize

Operator workflow when a row returns: start from the drilldown URL in the result (the example SPL builds a server inventory deep link) and the Cisco serial lookup page for cross-checking field assets: https://www.intersight.com/an/admin/serial-number-lookup?serial=<serial> when serial is available. In the Intersight Operate area, open the device, move to the Firmware or Inventory tab, and compare running images against the policy and repository. If drift=yes and the approved_version column is higher than what is on the host, open Profiles, Firmware policy, and attach/schedule a compliant upgrade in the next maintenance window; include a reboot and validation checklist your storage team needs for IOM, FI, and NVMe devices. If approved_version in the lookup is lower than the running version, treat that as a catalogue drift—likely your CSV still references an old golden image, not a “too new to be safe” case—refresh the row from the change board or HCL and document who approved the exception. Do not “fix” the server first without a ticket, or you will mask incomplete CMDB work.

Escalation: critical rows combine drift with cve_count>=1; page the security operations bridge within one hour and attach the Cisco advisory identifiers your cve_count references. high rows are drift plus end_of_support_date in the past; route to the platform engineering queue with a 90-day replacement or uplift plan. medium is drift on still-supported code without recorded CVEs; a tracked ticket within 30 days is enough for most enterprises. If only end_of_support fire without version drift, treat as a lifecycle problem even when the running firmware still matches a retired catalogue entry—refresh the date column after vendor publishes.

Silence and change windows: add a change_window field to a parallel lookup, or a suppression macro keyed on server_moid and the maintenance window tag from your change system, for planned cutovers. For Fabric Interconnect and IOM rows, use the same suppression rules as for NX-OS style maintenance, but keep FI upgrades visible to the network team because data-plane risk differs from a single host BIOS bump. The drilldown column in the SPL is meant for the server path; for FI and IOM, mirror a second saved search that swaps the URL pattern to the network element inventory where your tenant maps those objects.

## Step 5 Continuous Improvement

Automate lookup refresh: a small scripted input or external job can pull the Supported Firmware Releases and related HCL endpoints your subscription exposes, then emit CSV through a review queue rather than direct production overwrite. Version the CSV in Git with a tag per quarterly bundle, run diff against the previous file, and attach the diff to the change record so auditors can see why cve_count and end_of_support_date moved. Pair that automation with a weekly scheduled report that shows the percentage of server_moid rows with drift=no for required components, trending line over 90 days, not only a one-night snapshot.

Enrich further by joining cve_count to a live NVD or Cisco PSIRT feed so counts reflect retired advisories, and push medium-or-higher results into your SOAR platform to open pre-approved change tasks with CMDB identifiers. If you add hclstatus sourcetype events later, you can post-process with an outer join on server_moid to annotate HardwareStatus and SoftwareStatus when you want a second opinion beyond the static CSV, keeping this UC as the version-equality backstop and the HCL stream as a softer advisory channel.
grandmaExplanation: We check that the computers and network switches in your data centres are running the exact software versions your team has approved, so small mismatches are caught before they grow into real outages, surprise audit notes, or avoidable security gaps.
hardware: In scope: Cisco UCS B200 M5 through M7 blades including B200 M5 and B480 M5; Cisco UCS C220 M5 through M7, C240 M5 through M7, and C480 M5 rack servers; Cisco UCS X210c M6 and M7 and X410c M7 X-Series server nodes; Cisco UCS 6400- and 6500-generation Fabric Interconnects including FI 64108, FI 6454, and FI 6536, plus 6300-series 6332 and 6332-16UP switches where they front UCS domains; IOM 2208XP, 2304, 2408, and 2424 line cards that pair with the listed Fabric Interconnects; and device-local NVMe and rotating drives when their firmware is represented as RunningFirmware in Intersight. Each device must be Intersight-claimed; hardware sitting only in local CIMC with no claim will not be evaluated here.
